- [2023-01-15] **Research has been conducted on the effectiveness of therapy in treating PTSD** — Numerous studies have been conducted to determine the effectiveness of various forms of therapy in treating post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD).
- [2023-01-15] **Common forms of therapy for PTSD include CBT, EMDR, and prolonged exposure therapy** — Some of the most common forms of therapy for PTSD include cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR), and prolonged exposure therapy.
- [2023-01-15] **CBT and EMDR are more effective than medication in reducing PTSD symptoms among military veterans** — A prominent study published in the Journal of the American Medical Association found that CBT and EMDR were both more effective than medication (sertraline) in reducing PTSD symptoms among military ve
- [2023-01-15] **Prolonged exposure therapy shows slightly greater improvement than CBT for sexual assault survivors** — A study published in the Journal of Traumatic Stress found that both prolonged exposure therapy and CBT were effective in reducing PTSD symptoms among survivors of sexual assault, with prolonged expos
- [2023-01-15] **Therapy for PTSD can cause discomfort or distress during sessions** — Some people may experience discomfort or distress during therapy sessions as they work through traumatic memories and emotions.
- [2023-01-15] **Exposure therapies may cause initial increases in PTSD symptoms** — Exposure therapies such as prolonged exposure therapy may cause initial increases in symptoms as the person is exposed to triggering situations or memories.
- [2023-01-15] **Temporary increases in symptoms are generally followed by long-term improvements in PTSD symptoms** — Research suggests that these temporary increases in symptoms are generally followed by long-term improvements in PTSD symptoms.
- [2023-01-15] **Finding a qualified therapist is crucial for PTSD recovery** — Finding a qualified therapist who specializes in treating PTSD can be a crucial step towards recovery.
- [2023-01-15] **National Center for PTSD provides a directory of PTSD treatment providers** — The National Center for PTSD provides a directory of PTSD treatment providers, including therapists, counselors, and other mental health professionals.
- [2023-01-15] **American Psychological Association offers psychologist locator service** — The American Psychological Association provides a psychologist locator service that allows you to search for licensed psychologists by location and specialty, including PTSD.
- [2023-01-15] **Psychology Today directory allows searching for therapists by various factors** — The Psychology Today directory allows you to search for therapists by location, specialty, insurance accepted, and other factors.
- [2023-01-15] **NAMI offers a helpline for information and support in finding mental health services** — The National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI) offers a helpline that you can call or text for information and support in finding mental health services, including therapy for PTSD.
- [2023-01-15] **Therapy for PTSD typically lasts several months to a year or more** — The duration of therapy for PTSD can vary, but it generally lasts from several months to a year or more depending on various factors.
- [2023-01-15] **CBT for PTSD typically takes between 10-20 sessions** — Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) for PTSD typically takes between 10-20 sessions, though it can be modified and extended depending on individual needs.
- [2023-01-15] **Prolonged Exposure Therapy typically consists of 8-15 sessions** — Prolonged Exposure Therapy typically consists of weekly or bi-weekly sessions, ranging from 8-15 sessions in total.
